## [00:00 - 03:04]

Hi, this is Don Crowther. Let's talk about the metrics you should be majoring for your webinar. So question what should you be majoring? Two answers. The first one is everything so that you're not blind to any opportunity for improvement. The second one is to say hey a base set of metrics and then expand from there. So those are two different schools of thought. I'll tell you. Ideally, I prefer you to be in the first one. Set up tracking on everything so that you've always got the data whether you use it or not. So what I'm going to recommend here in this webinar is a certain base set of metrics, which is like everything, but it's only a small fraction of what you could be measuring, but it's a lot of them. So there's a general principle here, which is you want to measure people's behaviors. In other words, what they did. So did they click on this email? Did they register for the webinar? Did they attend the webinar? Where in the webinar did they leave? Did they hear your offer? Did they watch the replay? All of those are behaviors, what they actually did. And then what happens is once we get those indications of what they did, then we look at the implications of those behaviors and the possible thought patterns that go through that entire process. So for example, let's say you found that people were clicking on every single one of your emails that you send out about this webinar and tell the fifth one and then 50% of the people just stop clicking. Well, you've got a possible thought pattern that's happened is that they were very interested until something happened at which point they no longer were interested after that. And so then you can go look at what happened and be able to guess as to what their thought patterns, their beliefs, their attitudes were about what it is that you're doing. That's the way metrics work is we can get hard data on how they behave and then we use that to try to figure out what they're thinking behind their behavior. One other piece that we tried to measure in this entire process is that the things that the people do, who do buy, that the non-buyers don't do and the opposite of that the things that the people who do buy don't do that the non-buyers do do. Lots of do's in that. But the theory is if you can identify this is the path,

## [03:05 - 06:08]

these are the things that the buyers did, then we try to push more people through that path because that path is proven to get good results. And of course in this entire process, we want to focus on the things that we can control and influence. So I'm going to give you a fairly long list of metrics that I recommend tracking and that's on purpose. And I realized I actually showed this to somebody and and their reaction to me was can't you just give me three numbers to track? That's so much easier. And my answer to that was sure I can do that. The problem is that looking at those three numbers, you'll then know whether the webinar worked or not. But you'll have no idea why it didn't work and how to improve it. And that's the problem that most people have when it comes to metrics because metrics sounds like math and many people just didn't like math when they were growing up. And so they prefer to avoid math and even even if it's as simple as I'd like to know how many people actually watch the webinar. They just don't want to do that. They just want to look at and see a bunch of cells landing in their bank account. And then they they were planning on getting ten cells landing their bank account. They get three and they say it didn't work. And I say, OK, what didn't work the webinar? And it's like, no, no, no, what part of the entire webinar funnel that's around it didn't work? Where did it fail? I have no idea. And so then what we get into is what do you think? And. Thoughts about what did work and what didn't work. Are pretty much useless. It's the data that shows. What was really going on? And wherever possible, I prefer to take what we think out of the equation so that we only focus on the data that is essential. For solving the problems because that data. That is the only way that you can get information on what to change. And in order to be able to get that data, you got to track lots of things. Now what will happen is in most situations, most of the numbers you track won't be useful for that particular situation. But five webinars down the road when you maybe made some some other tweaks. Now that one piece of data becomes useful. And so don't say, well, it wasn't useful in the past. And so I'm not going to track it anymore. No, you track everything because you never know which number is going to be useful in any given situation.

## [18:20 - 21:24]

And then I'm going to break it down to okay, add source was it Facebook or Google or Instagram or whatever. I'm going to look at ad campaign. I'm going to look at this term we call return on ad spend are OAS. Which is your total cells divided by your total ad sales. So if I spent $1000 on ads and I made $3000 in sales, my return on ad spend is 300%. Okay, so that gives that total cells 300 divided by ad spend. And then you take the result of that and you multiply it by one by 100 gives you your percentage. Okay, and then I'm going to look at return on ad sales by add source by campaign by strategy. So let's say I have some ads that are going to an opt in incentive that then the thank you page of that opt in incentive is is giving them the ability to register for the webinar. Okay, that's one strategy. Another group of people are just going directly to the webinar sign up page. Another one group of them are going to the opt in incentive and then there's three or four days in between before they get invited to the webinar registration page. That's three separate strategies. I'm going to look at the return on ad spend for each one of those as well as ad copy and targeting. All right, now it's important that if you send traffic to your webinar from ads and you also send traffic to that particular webinar from your list or website traffic. You need to isolate out the people who came from the ads for the purpose of this ad spend analysis. The reason why is you don't want to inflate the effectiveness of your ads by giving them credit for sales that came through other sources. So let's just imagine that you spend $1,000 you got $3,000 in sales and you go, man, these ads are working great. I've got a three to one for every $1 I spend. I get $3 in revenue. All right, but what if half that traffic, what if half those sales came from traffic that never even saw an ad? They were on your list or whatever. So what that does is it actually takes the effectiveness of the ads from 300% divides that in half down to 150%. And it's like, okay, now those ads are not as powerful, not as effective as they were.

And you've got the data to be able to make decisions in the future. So just make sure you get the right data. It needs to be set up properly in Google analytics. And you probably if you don't understand what I just said, you probably should just go get somebody technical to support you on this. I'll just give you a real quick explanation of what this is. So let's imagine that your website is on WordPress, you're doing running your webinar from we'll just call it webinar jam. Call it webinar jam. Okay. And your shopping cart is on. I don't know any one of the shopping cart systems. Well, here's the problem. If you just put your Google analytics code on each of those three sites. They don't know Google doesn't know that that person came from one of the other ones before they got there. And so what it does is it calls each one of those a separate individual when an actual actuality it's the same individual clicking on a site in all three of those domains. And so cross domain tracking says, Hey, when you see somebody come from the website and land on the webinar software, double check Google analytics to see whether or not that's the same person. If it is, call it all one visit. Okay, so I'm just telling you this, because this is I find this mistake constantly when I look at other people's data, I look at it and I say, Oh, you don't have cross domain tracking set up properly, and it just makes the data become significantly less useful. ## [30:42 - 33:20]

You should know that data becomes more valuable. First off, when it's accurate. Now I realize that that is an understatement, but inaccurate data, not very valuable. And so always check double check and ask whether each number makes sense. So it is again, that's fairly astounding to me the number of times I look at somebody's data and I got this doesn't make sense. You shouldn't have a bigger number here than over there. And I realize that there's a lot of experience that goes in my being able to say that. But a lot of that came from just asking questions. Does this make sense? If it doesn't go check and double make sure that you're really gathering the data properly. You're not over counting and you're not under counting also data also becomes more valuable when you have more of it. So data on 1000 people is more valuable than data on 100 people. And so when I'm able to see, you know, if I'm only looking at 30 people going through the webinar, I'm not going to be very confident in that data that I'm seeing. Okay. And when it's confirmed when I see similar data coming from multiple different showings of the same webinar and the same funnel elements, that starts to confirm that the data that I saw before is accurate and tells me, Hey, I've either got a problem or I've got an opportunity or I've got a great thing going here. And I should go look at them more carefully. Recommendation. I recommend that you sit down with your team, or if your team is just you, you sit down with yourself and debrief each webinar. So we've talked about doing this before, but that was more of a qualitative thing. In this one, this is a quantitative. We're actually looking at its data and saying, What is the data indicate that you did well? What is the data indicate that you could improve and then go make those changes and do more of the things you did well so that you can get even better results in the future. That's metrics. It's not really hard. You just have to get it set up and then start looking at it in these debrief sessions. And it can become a very, very powerful tool that enables you to get a leg up on your competitors, as well as to actually know what's going on so you can make good decisions.
